questions.q: What is the primary goal of unit testing?
options: To test the entire application end-to-end
To test individual components or functions in isolation to ensure they work correctly
To verify that the database is properly configured
To measure application performance under load
questions.explanation: Unit testing focuses on testing small, isolated units of code (functions, methods, classes) to verify their correctness. This catches bugs early and enables safe refactoring. Option 0 describes end-to-end testing, option 2 is database validation, and option 3 is performance testing.

questions.q: Integration testing differs from unit testing by testing what aspect of the system?
options: Individual functions in complete isolation
The interaction and integration between multiple components or modules
Only the user interface layer
The performance of individual database queries
questions.explanation: Integration testing verifies that multiple components work together correctly, testing interfaces and interactions between modules. This validates that units integrate properly. Option 0 is unit testing, option 2 is UI testing, and option 3 is database performance testing.

options: Individual function logic in isolation
Database schema correctness
The entire application workflow from start to finish as a user would experience it
Code coverage percentages
questions.explanation: End-to-end (E2E) testing validates complete user workflows and scenarios through the entire system, including UI, business logic, and data. This ensures the application works from a user's perspective. Options 0 and 1 test specific layers, and option 3 is a code quality metric.

options: Writing tests after implementing all production code
Writing tests only for critical code paths
Writing tests before writing production code, then implementing code to pass the tests
Testing code in production to catch real-world issues
questions.explanation: TDD follows the red-green-refactor cycle: write failing tests first, then implement code to pass them. This approach improves design and ensures all code is testable. Option 0 is traditional testing, option 1 is selective testing, and option 3 is production monitoring.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of mocking in unit tests?
options: To slow down test execution deliberately
To replace external dependencies with controlled substitutes to test code in isolation
To automatically fix failing tests
To generate random test data
questions.explanation: Mocking replaces external dependencies (APIs, databases, services) with controlled substitutes, allowing isolated unit testing without side effects. This enables testing in isolation and improves test speed. Option 0 is a drawback, option 2 is invalid, and option 3 describes test data generation.

questions.q: The test pyramid suggests which distribution of tests is ideal?
options: Many end-to-end tests, few unit tests, moderate integration tests
Many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, few end-to-end tests at the top
Equal distribution across all testing types
Mostly integration tests with minimal unit and end-to-end tests
questions.explanation: The test pyramid recommends many fast unit tests (base), fewer integration tests (middle), and few slow end-to-end tests (top). This balances coverage with speed and cost. Options 0, 2, and 3 represent inefficient distributions that either waste time or miss bugs.

options: How much of the codebase is executed by tests
The speed at which tests execute
How many bugs exist in the code
The number of lines of code in production
questions.explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of code executed during testing, helping identify untested paths. High coverage doesn't guarantee quality, but low coverage indicates risk. Option 1 is execution speed, option 2 is bug counting, and option 3 is codebase metrics.

questions.q: Which testing practice is NOT part of continuous integration?
options: Running automated tests on every commit
Building code automatically after each push
Manually testing the application once a month
Failing the build if tests don't pass
questions.explanation: CI requires automated, continuous testing on every change. Monthly manual testing is neither continuous nor automated. Options 0, 1, and 3 are essential CI practices that provide rapid feedback on code quality.

questions.q: What is a stub in testing terminology?
options: A test that always passes regardless of code
A dummy implementation that returns pre-defined values without executing real logic
A tool for measuring test coverage
A bug in the test code itself
questions.explanation: A stub is a minimal implementation that returns hard-coded values, used to isolate code from external dependencies. Stubs differ from mocks in that they don't verify interactions. Option 0 is a bad test, option 2 is a coverage tool, and option 3 is a code bug.

questions.q: Why is it important to have both positive and negative test cases?
options: To balance the number of lines in test files
Positive tests verify expected behavior, while negative tests verify the system handles errors and invalid input correctly
Negative tests run faster than positive tests
They are required by law in some jurisdictions
questions.explanation: Comprehensive testing requires both positive cases (happy path) and negative cases (error conditions, invalid input). This ensures robustness and proper error handling. Options 0 and 2 are incorrect motivations, and option 3 is implausible.
